title: Add static branch predictor (backward-taken, JAL-always-taken)
motivation:
The current 5-stage core incurs a 1-cycle bubble on every taken branch and JAL
because IF always speculatively fetches PC+4. CoreMark is loop-heavy, so backward
branches and function calls are frequent. A static predictor that fetches the
target for backward branches and JAL eliminates the bubble on the common case,
improving IPC with negligible LUT cost (~50 LUTs) and no impact on the critical
path.

a BRANCH with a negative immediate (backward loop) or an unconditional JAL, the
PC is updated to the computed target instead of PC+4. A 1-bit pred_taken flag is
threaded through the IF/ID and ID/EX bundles. The EX stage suppresses redirect
when the prediction was correct; it only asserts redirect (triggering IF/ID flush)
on mispredict. JALR remains unpredicted. This saves one cycle per correctly
predicted taken branch or JAL.

title: Static BTFN branch predictor + JAL prediction in ID stage
motivation:
The current 5-stage pipeline resolves all control-flow instructions in EX and pays a
fixed 2-cycle bubble (flush IF/ID) for every taken branch and every jump. CoreMark is
loop-heavy; backward branches are taken the vast majority of the time. A static
Backward-Taken / Forward-Not-Taken (BTFN) predictor combined with unconditional JAL
prediction, both computed in the ID stage, eliminates the 2-cycle penalty for the
most common control-flow cases at virtually zero area cost.

hypothesis.motivation:

prediction, both computed in the ID stage, eliminates the 2-cycle penalty for the

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a simple static predictor in the ID stage that:
1) Predicts JAL (unconditional jump) always taken.
2) Predicts conditional branches as taken iff the immediate is negative (backward).
3) Redirects the IF PC and flushes the combinational IF/ID output in the same cycle
   the branch/jump is in ID.  A `predict_taken` bit is carried through ID/EX so that
   EX only asserts `redirect` on a mispredict (predicted-not-taken but taken, or
   predicted-taken but not-taken).  JALR is intentionally *not* predicted because its
   target depends on `rs1`, which may still be pending from an in-flight ALU result
 (forwarding only exists in EX, not ID).

This turns the common-case loop-back branch from a 2-cycle penalty into 0 cycles,
and unconditional JAL from 2 cycles to 0 cycles, while keeping forward branches and
JALR at their existing penalties.

title: MEM-to-EX load bypass to eliminate load-use stalls
motivation:
The current 5-stage pipeline stalls for one cycle on every load-use hazard
because it can only forward loaded data from MEM/WB, not from the MEM stage
itself.  CoreMark pointer-chasing and array-index patterns frequently emit a
LOAD followed immediately by a dependent ALU or STORE.  Eliminating the
load-use bubble improves IPC with no extra pipeline stages or tables.

title: Add 4-entry Return Address Stack for JALR return prediction
motivation:
The existing static predictor eliminates bubbles for backward branches and
unconditional JAL, but every JALR (function return) still incurs a 1-cycle
flush because the target is unknown until EX. CoreMark contains frequent
nested function calls, so returns are a noticeable fraction of the dynamic
instruction stream. A tiny Return Address Stack (RAS) pushes the link
address on JAL-to-x1/x5 and pops it on JALR-from-x1/x5, predicting the
return target in IF with high accuracy at negligible area cost.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Extend the IF-stage predictor with a 4-entry RAS. When the fetched
instruction is a JAL with rd=x1 or x5, push PC+4 onto the stack. When the
fetched instruction is a JALR with rs1=x1 or x5 and the stack is non-empty,
pop the top entry and treat it as the predicted target (pred_taken=1,
pred_target=ras_top). Thread the predicted target through the IF/ID and
ID/EX bundles so that EX can verify it. In EX, a JALR only causes a
redirect when it was unpredicted (!pred_taken) or when the actual target
differs from pred_target. The RAS pointer is updated only on non-stalled,
non-flushed cycles. This removes the bubble on the vast majority of
returns.

title: Eliminate false load-use stalls via opcode-aware hazard detection
motivation:
The current hazard_unit conservatively checks both rs1 (instr[19:15]) and rs2
(instr[24:20]) of the instruction in ID against the LOAD's rd in EX,
regardless of whether the instruction actually reads those fields as registers.
For I-type (OP-IMM, LOAD, JALR), U-type, and J-type instructions, the rs2
field is an immediate/shamt, not a register address. A LOAD followed by an
ADDI where imm[4:0] happens to match the LOAD's rd currently triggers a
spurious one-cycle stall. CoreMark uses small immediate values extensively,
so false stalls on common x1-x5 register numbers are likely frequent.
Eliminating these false stalls improves IPC with negligible LUT/Fmax cost.

Add opcode decoding to hazard_unit so load-use detection only fires when
the instruction in ID actually reads rs1/rs2 as registers. R-type, I-type
(OP-IMM/LOAD/JALR), S-type, and B-type use rs1; R-type, S-type, and B-type
use rs2. U-type and J-type use neither. This removes false stalls without
altering pipeline structure or lengthening critical paths.

title: Add 32-entry tagged BTB with 2-bit saturating counters for branch target caching and forward-branch learning
motivation:
The existing static predictor correctly predicts backward branches and JAL,
but forward branches that are frequently taken are always mispredicted.
Additionally, the combinational branch-target computation in the IF stage
(opcode decode → immediate extraction → sign-extension → PC adder) may
contribute to the critical path. A small Branch Target Buffer can learn
taken forward branches, cache targets to shorten the IF-stage path, and
coexist safely with the existing static BTFN predictor.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 32-entry direct-mapped Branch Target Buffer in the IF stage.
Each entry stores a valid bit, a 5-bit tag (PC[11:7]), a 30-bit
word-aligned target, and a 2-bit saturating counter.  In IF, a BTB
hit on a branch or JAL supplies the target directly from a register
read, bypassing the combinational immediate+adder path.  Direction
prediction remains static-taken for backward branches and JAL, but
the BTB can additionally predict taken for forward branches once
their counter reaches 2.  In EX, every resolved branch/JAL writes
its outcome and target back into the BTB.  JALR is excluded from
BTB usage to avoid aliasing noise.  This should improve IPC on
taken forward branches and may raise Fmax by reducing IF-stage
combinational delay.

title: Move JALR target resolution to ID stage to cut penalty from 2 to 1 cycle
motivation:
JALR is currently unpredicted in IF and resolved in EX, incurring a 2-cycle
misprediction bubble on every function return and indirect call. CoreMark's
list benchmark makes frequent function calls through pointers, so JALR
bubbles are a real IPC limiter.  Because the return address register (x1)
is almost always in the regfile when the JALR reaches ID (no forwarding
needed), we can compute the target address in ID stage and redirect IF one
cycle earlier, shrinking the penalty to a single flushed fetch slot.

title: Add 32-entry 2-bit BHT for forward branch direction prediction
motivation:
The existing IF-stage static predictor (backward-taken + JAL-always-taken)
already eliminated the largest source of control bubbles, yielding a +14.6%
fitness gain. The remaining dynamic mispredictions come mainly from forward
conditional branches that are frequently taken (e.g. early exits, loop-internal
conditionals) and from JALR returns. A prior 4-entry RAS improved IPC by only
0.3% because returns are rare, while a 32-entry tagged BTB broke in cosim.
A saturating-counter BHT attacks the much larger forward-branch bubble source
with minimal area (64 flops) and no changes to pipeline bundles.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 32-entry direct-mapped Branch History Table with 2-bit saturating
counters to the IF stage. The table is indexed by PC[6:2] and initialized to
strongly-taken (2'b11) so backward loops are correct immediately without any
training cost. For every conditional branch, the BHT entry MSB drives
predict_taken; unconditional JAL continues to be always predicted taken. In
EX stage, each resolved branch writes its actual outcome back to the entry
indexed by the branch PC using a standard saturating update rule. This adds
only 64 flops and a few LUTs of combinational logic, does not widen any
pipeline register, and the BHT read is off the critical instruction-fetch path
because the PC is known at the start of the cycle while the opcode decode
(which selects the BHT result) happens later in parallel.

title: Expand BHT to 128 entries to reduce aliasing among forward branches
motivation:
The existing 32-entry 2-bit BHT recovered +7.3% fitness by learning forward
branch directions, but with only 32 entries branches whose lower PC bits
collide alias destructively. In the 6 KB CoreMark working set this is not
uncommon, leaving a noticeable fraction of forward-branch bubbles unresolved.
Expanding the table to 128 entries (index PC[8:2]) keeps the proven
saturating-counter structure intact while cutting aliasing, at a cost of
only 192 additional flops and minimal LUT overhead.

title: Fold WB mux into MEM stage and remove dead EX/MEM fields
motivation:
The MEM/WB->EX forwarding path currently includes the combinational WB-stage
mux (mem_to_reg ? read_data : alu_result). Because fwd_mem_wb is driven by
wb_w_data, every instruction that forwards from MEM/WB pays the delay of this
32-bit 2:1 mux on top of the MEM/WB register Q, forwarding comparison, and
EX operand mux.  On a low-end FPGA this extra mux depth can be the difference
between timing closure and a slight Fmax loss.  Additionally, ex_mem_t carries
branch_taken and branch_target fields that are written in EX but never read
downstream, wasting ~33 flops.

title: Replace combinational divider with iterative multi-cycle unit
motivation:
The current ALU infers a full 32-bit combinational divider from the SystemVerilog
`/` and `%` operators. Flattened synthesis shows the EX stage alone consumes over
6000 cells (52 % of the entire core) and is dominated by arithmetic primitives.
Division is essentially unused in CoreMark, yet the divider consumes a massive
amount of area and routing resources that congest the fabric around the critical
ALU adder path. Removing the combinational divider should relieve routing
congestion and raise Fmax with negligible IPC impact.

title: Add 5-bit GHR gshare indexing to existing 32-entry BHT
motivation:
The 32-entry bimodal BHT already recovers +7.3% fitness, but destructive
aliasing among branches that share PC[6:2] limits further gains. A prior
attempt to fix aliasing by growing the table to 128 entries regressed -3%
because the larger read mux hurt Fmax. Gshare attacks the same aliasing
problem without enlarging the table: XORing a 5-bit Global History
Register (GHR) into the index scatters colliding branches while keeping
the 32:1 mux (and thus Fmax) essentially unchanged. Correlated branches
in CoreMark (e.g. state-machine checks, nested loop conditionals) also
benefit from history-aware prediction that a plain bimodal table cannot
learn.

title: Precompute forwarding selects in ID stage to shorten EX critical path
motivation:
The EX-stage critical path currently includes the forward_unit's 5-bit
address comparison, priority encoder, and operand forwarding mux before the
ALU.  On a LUT-based FPGA these extra logic levels consume precious
nanoseconds.  By shifting the forwarding decision into the ID stage—where
the same comparison is performed against the instructions currently in EX
and MEM—we register the 2-bit select signals into the ID/EX pipeline
register.  This removes the comparison and control generation from the
EX-stage datapath without changing pipeline behaviour or IPC.

title: Replace combinational divider with iterative multi-cycle unit
motivation:
The current ALU infers a full 32-bit combinational divider from the
SystemVerilog `/` and `%` operators. Flattened synthesis shows the EX
stage alone consumes over 6000 cells (52 % of the entire core) and is
dominated by arithmetic primitives. Division is essentially unused in
CoreMark, yet the combinational divider consumes massive area and
routing resources that congest the fabric around the critical ALU adder
path, limiting Fmax. A prior attempt at this change failed because the
divider state machine lacked a proper IDLE/RUNNING/DONE FSM with a
'started' bit, causing deadlock. With a correct FSM implementation,
removing the combinational divider should relieve routing congestion
and raise Fmax with negligible IPC impact.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Extract DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU out of the combinational ALU into a new
`divider.sv` module that performs restoring division in 33 cycles.
The divider MUST use a proper 3-state FSM (IDLE, RUNNING, DONE) with
a registered 'started' bit:
  - IDLE:  waiting for start pulse. busy=0, done=0.
  - RUNNING: counting down 32 bits. busy=1, done=0.
  - DONE: result valid for one cycle. busy=0, done=1. Next clock
    returns to IDLE automatically.
In `ex_stage`, detect divide ops and assert `div_start` for exactly
one cycle when the divider is in IDLE. The hazard unit stalls IF,
ID, and EX/MEM while `div_busy` (= RUNNING) is high. Because `busy`
is LOW in the DONE state, the EX/MEM register naturally captures the
divider result on the done cycle and the next instruction can enter
EX on the following cycle. The main ALU keeps all I-type ops and the
multiplier path. This structural change shrinks the EX stage
significantly, frees routing capacity on the critical path, and
should improve median Fmax without hurting CoreMark IPC.

title: Consolidate ALU multipliers to reduce routing congestion and improve Fmax
motivation:
The current ALU unconditionally computes three separate 64-bit products
(mul_ss, mul_uu, mul_su) on every cycle even though only one multiply
op is ever active.  On FPGA this causes the synthesis tool to infer
multiple wide multipliers and large cross-products that consume DSP
blocks and routing resources around the EX stage.  CoreMark is
multiply-heavy (matrix operations), so the ALU multiplier path is
likely on or near the critical path.  By sharing a single 64-bit
multiplier and selecting the signedness mode per-op, we cut the
multiplier area by roughly two thirds and relieve routing
congestion, which should raise median Fmax with zero IPC impact.

title: Word-load bypass from MEM to EX stage
motivation:
Prior MEM-to-EX load bypass failed because the critical path through
variable-shift + sign-extend degraded Fmax enough to cancel IPC gains.
Word loads (LW) are the dominant load type in CoreMark pointer chasing
and need no byte-alignment or sign-extension. A bypass that forwards
raw dmem_rdata straight from MEM to EX for LW-only dependencies keeps
the bypass wire path very short, potentially recovering the IPC gain
without the Fmax loss that killed the full-width bypass.

The fitness contract injects ~22% random backpressure on the instruction
bus.  Today every !imem_ready cycle asserts a global stall_if / flush_if
that freezes the PC and forces a NOP bubble into ID/EX.  A tiny 2-entry
skid buffer decouples fetch from issue: when the downstream pipeline is
stalled (load-use, dmem) but the bus is ready, the buffer prefetches
ahead; when the bus subsequently stalls but the buffer is non-empty, the
core continues issuing without bubbles.  This is a pure structural
decoupling that should raise effective fetch throughput with only
~130 flops and no new long combinational paths.

title: Fix gshare by speculatively updating GHR in IF with mispredict restore
motivation:
The 32-entry bimodal BHT already yielded +7.3% fitness, but a follow-up
gshare variant regressed -6.7% because its GHR was updated non-speculatively
in EX. By the time a branch resolved, the next branch had already been
fetched with stale history, increasing destructive aliasing. CoreMark
contains short basic blocks with back-to-back branches where history
staleness is fatal. A speculative IF-stage update with EX-stage restore
fixes the staleness while keeping the table small and the read path fast.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a 5-bit Global History Register (GHR) to the IF stage and XOR it into
the existing 32-entry BHT index (gshare). The GHR is updated speculatively
whenever a conditional branch is fetched: ghr <= {ghr[3:0], predict_taken}.
The pre-update GHR value is snapshotted into a new 5-bit field in the
IF/ID and ID/EX bundles. In EX, when a conditional branch mispredicts
(redirect is asserted), the GHR is restored to {snapshot[3:0], actual_taken}
via a new feedback port to IF. On correct prediction no restore is needed.
The BHT write index uses pc[6:2] ^ snapshot so the counter update hits the
same entry that was read during fetch. This gives history-aware prediction
without enlarging the array and with negligible combinational delay (a
5-bit XOR on an existing mux path).

title: Add 1-entry instruction skid buffer in IF to hide imem stalls and prefetch during downstream stalls
motivation:
The cosim fitness model injects ~22% random imem stalls, and every !imem_ready
cycle currently asserts a global stall_if/flush_if that freezes the PC and
forces a NOP into ID/EX, wasting the fetch slot.  Worse, during load-use or
dmem stalls the PC also holds even when imem_ready is high, so a valid
instruction arriving on the bus is discarded.  A tiny 1-entry skid buffer
decouples fetch from issue: it captures prefetches while the downstream
pipeline is stalled, and replays them when the bus subsequently stalls.
This hides single-cycle (and occasionally multi-cycle) imem bubbles with
only ~66 flops and a few LUTs of mux logic, improving effective fetch
throughput without lengthening any critical path.

title: Replace mem_stage barrel shifter with explicit byte-lane selects
motivation:
The failed MEM-to-EX load bypass hypothesis identified the variable
barrel shifter (dmem_rdata >> (addr*8)) as part of the critical path
that degraded Fmax enough to cancel IPC gains.  The current mem_stage
computes this 32-bit shift for every memory operation, even though
CoreMark is dominated by word loads (LW) which need no shifting.
Yosys often infers a full barrel-shifter network for the variable
shift pattern, adding unnecessary LUT depth on the dmem read path.

title: Add 1-entry non-blocking store buffer to hide dmem backpressure on stores
motivation:
The hazard_unit currently stalls the entire pipeline on every !dmem_ready cycle
whenever any memory operation is in MEM. Stores are roughly 20-25% of the dynamic
instruction stream and the cosim model injects ~22% random dmem stalls, so about
5% of total cycles are wasted waiting for the store bus. Because stores do not
produce register results, the instructions behind them are almost always
independent and could continue executing if the store were decoupled. A 1-entry
store buffer captures a stalled store and lets the pipeline advance while the
buffer drains asynchronously, converting store-bus stalls into useful execution
cycles with minimal area and no new long combinational paths.

When a store reaches MEM and dmem_ready is low, the buffer captures the address,
data, and byte mask instead of asserting a global stall. The buffer continuously
retries the write whenever dmem_ready is high. The top-level dmem outputs are
muxed between the buffer (when valid) and the live MEM stage. The hazard_unit

  - Loads still stall on !dmem_ready; stores use the buffer instead of stalling.
  - Any memory operation (load or store) stalls while the buffer is valid, because
    the single port is busy with the buffered write.
  - Non-memory instructions pass through MEM unimpeded even while the buffer drains.
This structural decoupling should recover the cycles currently lost to store stalls.

title: Add 8-entry loop predictor for backward branch exits
motivation:
The 32-entry 2-bit BHT already captures forward branch directions and
backward-taken bias, but it cannot avoid mispredicting the final iteration
of every loop (predicted taken, actually not-taken). CoreMark contains
many small inner loops (list traversal, matrix rows, CRC updates) where
this exit misprediction wastes cycles. A dedicated loop predictor learns
the trip count of individual backward branches and predicts not-taken on
the last iteration, removing the only remaining systematic direction
mispredictions with only ~100 flops and no wider BHT mux.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add an 8-entry direct-mapped loop predictor table to the IF stage.
Each entry stores a tag, a trained iteration length, a speculative
down-counter, and a 1-bit confidence flag.
When a backward branch is fetched, if the entry is valid, tag matches,
and confidence is high, the predictor overrides the BHT:
  - down-counter > 0  → predict taken and decrement the counter.
  - down-counter == 0 → predict not-taken.
In EX, resolved backward branches update the table:
  - During training (confidence low) the table counts taken outcomes;
    the first not-taken exit stores the count as the trained length
    and sets confidence high.
  - On a correct exit (predicted not-taken) the counter is reloaded
    from the trained length for the next loop invocation.
  - On a length mismatch (mispredict) confidence is cleared so the
    entry re-trains on the next invocation.
This attacks the last predictable bubble source without touching the
existing BHT or pipeline bundles.

title: Hash-mix higher PC bits into 32-entry BHT index to reduce aliasing
motivation:
The 32-entry 2-bit saturating-counter BHT is the proven sweet spot for
this core, delivering a +7.3% fitness gain. A follow-up attempt to grow
the table to 128 entries regressed -3% because the wider read mux hurt
Fmax, while gshare global-history variants regressed due to history
staleness. The remaining occasional forward-branch mispredictions may
stem from destructive aliasing among branches that share the low five
PC bits. A zero-cost hash that XORs higher PC bits into the index
scatters colliding branches without enlarging the array or adding any
new state.

change both the read index and the write-back index from pc[6:2] to
pc[6:2] ^ pc[11:7]. In the IF stage this mixes bits 7-11 of the PC
into the existing 5-bit index, reducing the chance that two branches

hits exactly the entry that was read during fetch. The combinational
cost is a single 5-bit XOR, which Yosys will absorb into the existing
LUT fabric with negligible Fmax impact. No new flops, pipeline bundles,
or control signals are introduced.

title: Exclude backward branches from BHT to eliminate aliasing pollution on forward branches
motivation:
The current 32-entry 2-bit BHT is indexed by PC[6:2] and updated for every
resolved conditional branch. Backward branches (loops) are almost always
taken and saturate their entries to 2'b11 almost immediately. When a
forward branch happens to alias with a hot backward branch, the backward
branch’s constant "taken" updates keep overwriting the forward branch’s
state, preventing the forward branch from ever training to its true bias.
This destructive aliasing is the largest remaining accuracy loss in the
32-entry table, yet it can be fixed with zero extra hardware: backward
branches can be predicted statically taken (they already are) and simply
omitted from the BHT update path, freeing their index slots exclusively
for forward branches.

Prior attempts to reduce aliasing (128-entry BHT, gshare, hash-mixing)
all failed because they enlarged combinational paths or added flop state.
This approach removes updates, adds no state, and shortens no paths — it
only improves the *quality* of the information stored in the existing 32
entries.
